respStmt.name: Dirk Van Hulle
respStmt.resp: SBDMP coordinator, encoding scheme, TEI customization

respStmt.name: Vincent Neyt
respStmt.resp: Project architecture, encoding scheme, TEI customization

publicationStmt.authority: Centre for Manuscript Genetics (University of Antwerp)

availability.p: Not to be distributed outside the project

sourceDesc.p:
Abbreviated in the Manuscript Chronology as EM
Manuscript notes in an exercise book by Samuel Beckett which represent the first draft of Krapp's Last Tape. Contained in an exercise book which is inscribed 'Eté 56' on the front cover by Beckett. 96 leaves, blank from f. 45 until f. 96 verso. Squared paper. 22 x 13 cm. Blue and black ink.

From f. 10 verso to f. 22 and then on verso f. 24 and f. 25 this exercise book contains the original draft of what became Krapp's Last Tape, here headed by Beckett as Magee Monologue (a reference to actor Patrick Magee for whom Beckett originally wrote this piece, and who played Krapp in the first production of the play.) Krapp is referred to as 'A'. Piece is dated f. 11, 20 February 1958.

The sequence of pages includes two separate sections of the piece, the first beginning on f. 11 with some notes on setting and on physical and verbal characterisation, the second beginning on f. 14 with no preliminary notes. Variant material in content as well as text; opening mime includes (on f. 12) a scene in which Krapp finds the already laden table to be unsteady. He moves the table, and the 'zone of light' follows him. He does not remark upon this. The girl with whom Krapp lived 'on and off' is called 'Alba' in this draft, recalling Belacqua's Alba and Beckett's poem of that title.

The verso pages are used primarily for corrections and notes. There are also many doodles adjacent to pauses or revisions. Some calculations related to the time spent 'on licensed premises alone'. The final pair of pages, verso p. 24 and recto p. 25, carry notes and diagrams on 'Tape recording'. These notes concern Krapp's position relative to the machine and how he would operate, listen and newly record upon it.

Bryden, Mary, Julian Garforth, and Peter Mills, eds. (1998), Beckett at Reading: Catalogue of the Beckett Manuscript Collection at the University of Reading (Reading: Whiteknights Press and the Beckett International Foundation), p. 58-59

p.seg:
-
For
Reading University
Library
Sam. Beckett
-

p.seg: Front centre,

sitting on a small
plain wooden chair at before a small
plain wooden table, X.
About 70 .

seg.del:
from antiquated clothes. Coat and
trousers of different suits.

p.seg:
Red rusty baggy
Rusty black narrow trousers, ⁁ too short for him.

Black
xxx
Rusty black sleeveless waistcoat, 4 pockets.

Grimy white
shirt open at neck. No collar.

Surprising pair of yellow boots
white cricket boots,
size ten, very narrow & pointed.
Pallid face.
Purple nose.
Very near-sighted and

hard of
hearing.
No glasses.

On the table a tape-recorder and
a number of cardboard boxes
containing recorded tapes.

Table and immediately adjacent area
in strong light.

Rest of stage in shadow
darkness.

seg.stage: (reading from ledger, his nose down on it).

p.seg: Box three Box ... thrrree ... spool ... five.
p.stage:
( He turns his head front without rai
and stares front.
With relish.)
p.seg: Spool!
Spooool!

seg.stage:
(He turns back to
table, starts peering & poking
at the boxes.)

p.seg:
Box ... three
thrree
thrree ... thrree ... four... two ...
(with surprise) ... nine! ... seven...
good God! ... seven ... ah! ....
the little rascal!

seg.stage:
(He separates box
three from the others.)

p.seg: Box three thrree.

seg.stage:
(He opens it, peers at the spools it
contains.)

p.seg:
Spool ... (He peers at
ledger, finds the entry.) Five. (He
peers at spools) Five ... five ...

seg.stage:
(He
takes out a spool, xx straightens up,
holds up the spool between finger
& thumb.)

p.seg: Box thrree, s Spoool five.
wearish small old man,

almost
blind

( x objects, writing up against
eye),

almost deaf (cupped ear down
almost touching tape),

sitting
centre on small plain wooden chair
at small plain wooden table.

On table: a thick worn ledger,
a tape-recorder and a number
of cardboard boxes filled
containing recorded
tapes.

Table and immediately adjacent
zone in a circle of strong light,

rest
of stage in shadow.

seg.stage: (reading from ledger, his nose down on it)

p.seg: Box ... three ... spool ... five
p.stage:
( He turns his head from ledger
away from table
xxx xxx
front,
without raising it.
Appreciatively.
)
p.seg: Spoool!
Spooool!

seg.stage:
(He turns back to table,
starts peering at the boxes) and
fumbling at the boxes.)

p.seg:
Box ... three.
(He turns his head front without raising
it. As before, ). The rolling the r). Three!
(Pause) Thrreee! (He turns back
to boxes) Box ..... box ...... (peering
at numbers) ......... four ......... two .....
(with surprise) ... nine! .... seven ...
ah! the little rascal!

seg.stage:
(He
separates the box from the others)

p.seg: Box three thrreee.

seg.stage:
(He opens it,
peers at the spools it contains)

p.seg:
Spool
... (He turns back to peer at ledger,
peers at it, finds the entry.) Five.
(He turns back to peer at spools.) Five ...
five ...

Spool
five.

seg.stage:
(He places spool on table before
him, closes box and puts it back to
with the others. He turns back to spool.)

p.seg: Box thrreee spoool five.
p.stage:
( He puts it
on the machine. )
He x peers again at
ledger. Finally. )
p.seg: Passim.
p.stage:
( He turns
head front without raising, stares

stage.seg:
(He lays it on table, closes box three
and puts it back with the others. He
bends over spool.

p.seg:
Box three Box thrree,
spool five.
p.stage:
( He puts spool on machine,
peers at ledger. Reading from ledger
entry at foot of page. )
p.seg:
Mother at
rest ...
The black ball ...
The black ball? ??

... The
dark nurse
... broods ...

seg.stage:
(He raises
his head, broods, peers again at ledger)

p.seg: Improvement of bowel condition ...
Memorable ... what?
Equinox. Memorable equinox.
p.stage:
( He raises his head, stares xx front.
Puzzled. )
p.seg: Memorable equinox?
p.stage:
( Pause.
He shrugs his shoulders, peers again at
ledger. )
p.seg:
Farewell ... to - (he turns
the page) - love.

seg.hi:
He closes ledger, bends over machine,
switches it on, takes up listening
posture, i.e. ear cupped towards
ear cupped towards machine, face
front.

seg.stage:
(strong young voice, but unmis
takably his at a much earlier time.)

p.seg: Thirty-seven today, sound -

seg.hi:
Settling himself more comfortably
he knocks a box off the table
on the floor. He curses, stops
machine, sweeps boxes and
ledger violently off table,
switches on the machine, resumes
his pose.

p.seg: Thirty-seven today etc.
P Passim! The P is overwritten in black ink (the rest of the word still in blue ink; from the next word onwards, the text is written in black ink.

seg.stage:
(He turns back to
table, starts up machine, crouches
down over tape, cupping his ear The second letter of the word is unclear; it almost seems as if Beckett (perhaps inadvertently) wrote "eye" instead of "ear".,
his face turned front.

(strong young voice, unmistakably A's
at a much earlier time).

p.seg:
This day,
being in the third - xxx

seg.hi:
Settling himself more comfortably
A knocks a box off the table on to
the floor. He curses, stops the
machine, sweeps boxes and
ledger violently off the table,
turns
⁁
turns back tape to beginning starts up the machine again,
resumes his pose.

p.seg:
This day This day, being in the
third -

seg.hi:
The weight of boxes and ledger
off the table he feels it unsteady
on its legs, curses, stops the
machine, tries to steady it,
fails, gets up and carries it
laboriously towards front. This
brings him out of zone of light.

He sets down table in shadow,
goes back
into for chair, carries
it to table and sits down in
shadow. The light moves
and comes to rest on him in
his new position.

He is now
say a yard or so from edge
of stage. He makes sure
the table is firmly on its
legs, turns back tape to
beginning, resumes his
position.

p.seg:
This day, being in the
third decade of the Ram,
I enter upon my 31 st
year, sound apparently in wind and
limb, apart from my old
trouble, and intellectually,
I have little doubt, at the
height
peak of my powers.

(Pause.
With fervour.) May the all
all-merciful Providence -
(Pause. With fervour. Continues
)

May
the star which shone on
my cradle in xxx Great
stood above my cradle
bassinet in
North Great Georges Street
continue to burn, as heretofore,
in my breast and in my
labours.

(Pause. In a lower
voice.) In particular may I
be given the strength, in the
years coming years, to ...
(Hesitation. Finally, still lower .)
to drink less. (Pause.) Solemnly.)

In the
past year 12 months I spent, in l on licensed
premises alone, anything between from
seventeen and to nineteen hundred
hours. (Pause.) Nineteen Nineteen hundred
hours. (Pause.)

Oh I know, I know,
I have had these crises before, but
never
at no time quite so acutely as today
this afternoon, sitting
lying on the grass, in the park, in
the sun. (Pause.) What would help me
more than anything, I think, is a ...
fuller ... a ... a fuller ... a more ... more natural ...
a more ... absorbing
engrossing
(lowering his voice) ... sexual
life. (Pause.) ← arrow to indicate facingleaf addition
Enthusiastic.
Intercourse! with real partners.

(Pause.) These nineteen hundred hours,
for example, if in the future instead of having of
spent them bibul spending them
bibulating, I were to spend them -

A stops the machine,
then gets remains
motionless a moment, then gets up

p.seg: and goes backstage into shadow.

seg.hi: Noise of cork drawn with loud pop.

p.seg: 10 seconds pause.
Second cork.

Ten
seconds pause.

Sudden Brief burst of song
from A in shadow.
Pause.

x In a
kind of shuffling dance he reenters
zone of light, from an unexpected
angle, say front right, shuffles and
turns for a moment about the x
table, finally lets himself fall on
the chair. After a moment he bends
over the tape, winds it forward
a little, starts up machine, resumes
his listening attitude.

- a moment in the life of all
pioneers

Thirty-seven today, sound apparently in
wind and limb, apart from my old
trouble, and intellectually, I have
little doubt, at the peak of my powers,
or thereabouts.

Celebrated the occasion,
as in xxx recent years, quietly at
the Winehouse.
No one there I knew.

Sat by the fire
looking into the fire with closed eyes
turning over in my mind
winnowing, out as it went separating
the grain from the chaff.

Jotted
down a few notes, on the back of
an envelope.

It is good to be home
again, in my old things.

The new
light above my desk is a great
improvement.

With all this darkness
round me, I am less alone.
(Pause.)
In a way.
(Pause.)

I like to get 
up and move about in it, then come 
back here to...(
                           
                              he hesit he hesitates
                        )... myself.

The grain, what do I mean by that,
I mean ... (Pause.) I suppose I mean those

p.seg: Insufferable prig

p.seg:
things that will matter to me
worth the recalling having
20 years
xxx xxx xxx in later life
when all the dust has - this when
all this dust has settled.

I try to
be the old man who one day perhaps will
listen to this I close my eyes and try and
imagine them.

Extraordinary silence this evening
this evening. I
strain my ears and do not hear a sound.

seg.add:
Miss Williams Old Miss Williams always sings
at this hour.
I hope nothing has happened to her.

p.seg:
I have just listened to one of the old an old year,
tapes, passages at random.

I did not
check it in the catalogue
register, but it must
be at least ten or twelve years ago.

I was living
then with Alba , in xxx alley
At that time I was living off and on in ?
street.

I must say I - (A stops
machine, broods at length, starts machine
again.) - find it a great h
this a
great help before embarking on a new ..
er .. review review.

I find it hard to
believe that young man was I.

Even the
voice is unfamiliar.
Aspirations,
resolutions.
To drink less, in particular.

He speaks
of 17 hundred hours, out of the preceding
8000 odd, spent consumed on licensed premises
alone.
Plans for a fuller sexual life.

Last
illness of his father.

References to his life's
work.

Closing with a - (brief laugh) -
an appeal to Providence.

( Prolonged
laugh.)

What remains of all that
twaddle lumber,

a girl in a shabby green
coat, on a railway-station platform?
When I look -

A stops the machine, broods, gets up
and walks
goes backstage into the shadows .
Pause of 10 seconds.

Sound of a cork
drawn
with
popping.
Ten seconds.
Second pop.
Ten seconds.

A comes back into light from an un
expected angle, say front right ,
sits down, resumes his pose, switches
on machine.
back on the year that

bench by the lock
weir

p.seg:
is gone, with the old eyes to be, there is of
course xx
the house on the canal where
mother died lay dying, in the early autumn,
after her long widowhood, and the

bench by the weir where I used to sit,
day after day, watching her window, xx
wishing she were dead.
I was there when the blind went down,
throwing a ball for a dog I remember.
from where I could see her window.

I
used to sit there, day after day, wishing
she were dead gone.

Quite a number of
people I got to xx know then, by
appearances, nurses
nursemaids and children,
old men, and dogs.

add.seg:
One dark young woman I remember
particularly, with a big black pram.

Whenever
I look Day after day.

Whenever I looked
in her direction she had her eyes on me.

But when at last I spoke ventured to speak to her she
threatened to call a policeman.

p.seg:
I was there
when the blind went down, throwing
a ball for a dog as xx xxx it
happened. (A switches off, broods,
switches on again) - the blind went
down, the throwing a ball for a dog
as it happened.

I xx looked up and
there it was.

I x sat on for a few
minutes with the ⁁ ball in my hand and \'the\' probably accidentally omitteddog barking and pawing at me.

Then I held out the ball to me it out to me him and
he took it in his mouth, very gently.

An old
black
tennis ball it was , all black
and sodden, but not punctured.

I wonder is that the kind of thing.
how much there is there.

Creatively
Intellectually a disheartening year year of discouragement until
that unforgettable day night in March, ⁁ at the end of the pier, ⁁ in the high wind when
I suddenly saw the whole thing.

This, is
what I have I realized in the Winehouse,
is what I have chiefly to record this
evening against the day when all my
work is
will be done and perhaps no place in my place
left in my memory for of the wonder that
made it possible place left in my
memory, and no thankfulness
for the wonder that made it possible.

What I saw then was briefly this.
The principles on which had been
The assumption on which I had going

p.note: Whole paragraph crossed out with St. Andrew\'s cross in black ink
p.seg:
- strange association till my dying day
of storm and night with the light
of understanding and the peace -

p.seg: - clear to me at last that this

p.note: Whole paragraph is crossed out with a St. Andrew\'s cross in black ink.
p.seg:
.. foam flying
- granite rocks the foam
flying up in the light of the
beacon and the anemo anemometer
spinning like a propellor,
clear to me at last that
the dark I have struggled to
keep out of my work -
is in reality its true -

figure.ana: doodlecategories.xml#Object doodlecategories.xml#Musical-instrument doodlecategories.xml#Piano doodlecategories.xml#Shape doodlecategories.xml#Rectangle doodlecategories.xml#Triangle doodlecategories.xml#Square doodlecategories.xml#Hatching

p.seg: Perhaps my best years are over.
agreed it was hopeless
been going all these years, namely that -

(A switches xxx off machine, winds
tape forward, switches on again)

-
granite rocks the foam flying up in
the light of the beacon and the anemometer
spinning like a propellor, clear to me
at last that the dark I have struggled
to keep out of my work is in reality
its true -

(Curses, switches off, winds
forward, switches on)

- strange
association till my dying day of storm
and night with the light of understanding
and the peace -

(Curses louder, switches
off, winds forward, switches on..)

- my hand
across
face buried in her breasts and my
hand on her.
We lay there without moving.

But
underneath us all moved and moved us,
gently, up and down, and to and fro from side
to side.
Mi Past midnight.

I n Never re- knew
member such a silence.

The coming year will decide. May I be
given the strength and the -

seg.stage:
(A switches off, winds back tape,
switches on again.)

p.seg:
- upper lake, with the boat
punt, bathed
off the bank
bathed
off the bank,
then pushed out, to the island pushed
out into the stream and drifted.

She lay stretched
out on the floorboards with her hands
under her head and her eyes closed.

Hot sun, with a bit of a breeze and
the water nice and lively.

I noticed
a scratch on her thigh and asked her
when how she got it.

Picking gooseberries,
she said.

I said xxx I thought it
was hopeless and no good going on,
and she agreed, without opening her
xx eyes.

I asked her to look at me and
after a moment she did, but just slits, because
The eyes she had! .
p.stage:
( Broods. ↑ Realizes he is recording silence switches
off. Broods.)
p.seg:
Everything there,
everything, all the light and -
dark of the .. (hesitates) ages ,

seg.stage:
(realizes this is not being recorded,
switches on.)

p.seg:
Everything there,
every bleeding thing, all the light and
dark of ... (hesit the ... (hesitates)
ages.
Let that go!
Jesus!

Take
his mind off his exercises!
Jesus!

Ah well, maybe he was
right.
(Pause.)
Maybe he was right.
of the sun. glare.

I bent over her
to get them in the shadow and they
opened.

We drifted into the in among
a patch of reeds and got stuck.

The noise way they
made going went down under the blunt
xxx
blunt stem,
hissing.

I went down across her lay down
across her with my face in her breasts and
my hand on her.

We lay there without
moving.

But underneath us all moved,
and moved us, gently up and down, and to and
fro.
Past midnight.
Never knew -

stage.p:
( A switches off, sits brooding,
feels fumbles in his
pockets, takes out an envelope, fumbles,
puts back the envelope, gets up and
goes front right into shadows.
Ten
seconds.
Noise Sound of bottle against
glass, then brief siphon.
Then Ten
seconds.
Bottle against glass alone.
Ten seconds
Fifteen seconds.
He comes
back front left into light, with an
unused tape in his hand, sits down,
takes tape off machine and lays it
on table, puts tape in his hand on
machine, takes envelope from
his pocket, consults back of it, switches on
and begins to record.

p.seg:
Just been listening to that stupid
bastard I was 30 years ago, hard
to believe I was ever as bad as that.

del.unclear:
xxx xxx with them and xxx
xxx xxx, xxx xx xxx
xxx

p.seg: The eyes she had!
p.stage:
(
                           Broods,
 realizes he is recording silence, switches
 off. Broods.
                        )
p.seg:
Everything there, everything,
all the -

seg.stage:
(Realizes this is not being
recorded, switches on.)

p.seg:
Everything
there, everything every bleeding thing,

Croghan
Croghan

figure.ana: doodlecategories.xml#Shape doodlecategories.xml#Abstract-shape doodlecategories.xml#Rectangle doodlecategories.xml#Object doodlecategories.xml#Piano doodlecategories.xml#Hatching

p.seg:
all the light and dark of the ... (hesitates)
... ages.
Let that go!
Jesus!

Take
his mind off his homework!
Jesus!

Ah well, maybe he was
right.
Maybe he was right.
p.stage:
( Broods.
Switches off.
Consults
envelope.
Crumples it and throws it
away.
Broods.
Switches on.
)

p.seg: Not a goddamn thing to say.

What's a
year now?

Reverie and constipation obstipation.
The old trouble. The odd hard stool.

Seventeen copies sold, one pound
six and something, eight probably.
xxx Getting known.

Went
out a couple of times once or twice, in July or
August.

Sat in the park in the
middle of the brats and skivvies,
dreaming & wished
and wishing I were gone.

add.seg:
Swore to resist them
fight them off.

p.seg:
Read Effie
Burnt the eyes out of me reading Effie again, a couple of pages
a day, with tears again.

Could
have been happy with her, up there
on the cold sea, and the woods
pines, and
the dunes.

Cxxx Fanny
came in a couple of times.

Fat
old whore.

Couldn't do much, but
better than nothing.

The last time
wasn't so bad.

How do you do it,
she said, at your age?

I told her
I'd been saving up for her all
my life.
Went to vespers once. , like when I was a child.
Fast asleep. Slept.

Days when Sometimes wondered in the night
I wonder x if a last effort wouldn't
mightn't -

xx Empty the bottle now and
get to bed.
Finish this vomit tomorrow.
Or leave it at that.

Leave it
at that.

Lie propped up on my
back in the dark, and wander.

Be
again in the dingle on a Xmas Eve,
with my nanny, picking xx picking
holly.

Walk over
Be again on Croghan on a Sunday
And so on.
(Pause)

Perhaps
Here I end this tape.

Perhaps
my best years are over.

But
I wouldn't have want them back.

Not
with the xxx fire in me now.
No, I wouldn't have them back.

morning, in the snow, stop and listen
to the bells.
And so on.
Be again.

All that old rubbish
rubbish .
Once wasn't enough.
Lie down across her.
p.stage:
( Long pause.
He
suddenly bends to the machine, switches off wrenches
off tape, throws it away, puts on the
other, winds it to the passage he wants,
switches on, listens staring front.

p.seg: - gooseberries, she said.
Etc.
Past midnight,

never knew such a
silence.
Here I end this tape.

Perhaps my
best years are over.

But I wouldn't want
them back.
Not with the fire in me now.
No, I wouldn't have them back.

stage.seg: A sits motionless staring before him.
The tape runs on in silence.

p.note: Page crossed out with St. Andrew\'s cross

seg.unclear: with my nose in a ⁁ my little cloud

p.seg:
beg, borrow, steal, cadge or even
buy, and lend me posthaste,
a prospectus xx (directions for use)
of a tape-recorder.
p.add:
↑ Arrow to indicate addition I have written, involving this tape-
recor object, a stage mono. for Magee,
and want to must check up on the
xx mechanic mechanics of it
p.seg:
This seems is
as important as necessary to me at the moment
as the critique of thaw to frost
in Siberia.

I hear from Deryk Deryk Mendel that Fin de
Partie and mime are to be
performed, in conjoint xxx
tandem, at the Venice Biennale.
Possibly also at the Holland Festival.

Endspiel is xxx, mise en
scène x in Vienna the day after
tomorrow. Suzanne is off there
tomorrow. I stay here, with
my
noise nose
The word seems to read "noise"; probably this is meant to read "nose" (as in the first line on page 20v). in my little cloud.
For page I.
Well out of that.
Hopeless business.

Not much allusi
on reference to her. A queer passage about
her eyes.

I don't xxx remember what
colour they were. suddenly saw them
again.

Incomparable
Matchless.
Ah well.

seg.del:
I find these ... (He switches off,
broods, switches on) ...

p.seg: (Pause)

I find - (he switches off, broods,
switches on) - these old PMs P.M.s
a great help before embarking on a
new ... (hesitates) ... conspectus.

Hard
to believe I was ever that young pup.
Opening.

seg.unclear:
Consults register and
reads out rubrics.

p.seg: "Memorable equinox"

"Death of mother "Mother at
peace"

Improvement of bowel
condition -

Farewell to -
love
(he turns page) - love.

Will those who think of him today
a little simple prayer to xxx say.
gone, but not forgotten
He only takes the best

On earth you suffered, in heaven you rest,
God bless you, dear -, you were one
of the best
you always did your best for us

God needed a star in heaven,
A beautiful light to shine,
Out of this world of sorrow
He chose the fatter of swine
← Arrow to indicate addition. But why oh why did it have to be
That wonderful uncle of mine

Like falling leaves the years slip by
But memories of you will never die

We think of you in silence
And make no outward show
But what it means to lose you
Only God will ever know

Whatever else I fail to do
I shall never forget to pray for you

These words are written with deep regret
By a - that xx loved you and
will never forget

Pray for us, dear -
To Jesus Christ our king,
That he may bless our lovely home,
where once you dwelt therein

I often sit and wonder why
The thorns live on, but the roses die

Only a cluster of memories
Sprayed with a million tears.

Among the immovable masses
of a being set aside, closed
shut
pent up
and in for ever on itself,
pathless, airless, cyclopean
a flare of flames and
being
placi
lit fitfully xxx
the fit
the
colours of
the fitful fires of the spectrum of
dark.

An Endless unveiling
veil after
beyond veil, plane after
plane of imp imperfect
transparency, an unveiling
towards the unveilable, the
nothing, the thing again.

And
b
B urial in oneness
the only, in
the a space of
xxx with impenetrable
closeness
imminence
closeness, cell cell painted
on the xxx stone of cell,
art of incarceration.

The shock was sudden, the blow severe,
We little thought your end was near,
This only those who have lost can tell,
The sorrow of parting, dear Dad, without
farewell.

The wound is deep, it will not heal,
God only knows how we feel

These words are written with deep regret
By a wife who loved you & will
never forget

God takes the best for reasons divine
But your memory will live till the end of time

On Mary's beads we plead your needs
And in the holy mass
I love you, Pop, I love you yet

Guard him, o Mary, don't leave him
alone,
love him, dear Jesus, as we did at home.

God has a beautiful garden
For he only chooses the best

We think of him each morning,
We think of him at night,
Of all the pain he suffered,
God knows it was a fight.

Never did a heart more tender
Throb within a human breast

Hard was the xxx that compelled us to part
with our dear loved Mommy, so dear to our hearts

Bracken
Hickey
Murdy
Mongly
Foley
Weaver
Convoy
Tuite
Keeley
Wade

Leaden
Sote
Tisdall
Cobbe
Moult
Needham
Harney
McGlone
Siney

Thou art gone, the two, the whole,
And we mourn you in vain,
For those eyes that beamed with
lovelight
Ne'er shall rest on us again

Like falling xx leaves the years
roll by
But memories of you will never die

In a garden of rest, no worry, no pain,
God keep you, Mick, until we
meet again

A tireless worker, good and true,
One in a million, mother, were you.

seg.note: A sketch of the stage setting.
A sketch of the stage setting with a table and pool of light.

p.seg:
Should Kra Krapp move away
from table when standing?

To stool front, on which he has left
uneaten banana?

Then hasten back to put on
other reel?
Or stay at table for recording?

Do not remove lid of machine,
x
xxing thus unxxx
hidden from majority of
audience.

K. x only appears to load tape
on machine.

Actually it is
inxxx already ready loaded.

Thus
Similar he ⁁ only appears to change reels
when he wishes to record.

Actually he leaves in position the
tape
reel already loaded

Similarly at the end only he only appears to change
reels again.

Objection : The raised lid will
obscure Krapp's face
